text: The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position for a laparoscopic right adrenalectomy. The surgeon enters the abdomen in the right upper quadrant using a 5mm optically guided trocar.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection and bipolar electrocautery to carefully separate extensive adhesions between the adrenal tumor and the underlying liver capsule. The tumor does not appear to be grossly invading the liver capsule.

The surgeon carefully avoids grasping the adrenal tumor directly, instead using blunt retraction on the liver. The retroperitoneal space is entered using a curved bipolar electrocautery device, which aids in dissection and hemostasis. The dissection focuses on exposing the blood supply to the adrenal tumor, including the adrenal vein and the inferior vena cava.

The adrenal mass is visible, having been freed from its attachments to the liver and inferior peritoneal attachments. The surgeon avoids grasping the tumor directly, instead manipulating the pericapsular fat and attached peritoneal layers. A self-retaining liver retractor is used to keep the left lobe of the liver suspended in the surgical field for continued exposure.

The surgeon focuses on the vascular supply to the adrenal mass, utilizing blunt dissection, suction, sharp dissection, and electrocautery. The liver retractor is visible on the right, holding the liver aside, while a blunt grasper is used to pull the adrenal tumor to the left for better exposure.

The surgeon dissects around the medial portion of the adrenal tumor, identifying several feeding vessels originating from the direction of the inferior vena cava. These vessels are carefully ligated using bipolar cautery to prevent hemorrhage.

The surgeon separates the inferior margin of the adrenal mass from the adjacent kidney, revealing a clear plane between the two structures. A dominant vessel, identified as the adrenal vein, is clipped and divided using surgical clips.

The surgeon uses bipolar electrocautery to transect the remaining vessels. The adrenal tumor is visible on the right side of the screen. The lateral peritoneal attachments are severed, followed by the transection of the right triangular ligament and the superior attachments to the liver capsule.

The surgeon continues dissection both inferiorly and superiorly to release the adrenal tumor from all attachments. The tumor is fully mobilized, revealing a normal appearing adrenal gland, which is then excised.
